text: Literally, ‘I shall no more see man, nor the dweller in quiet’. The reference seems to be to the years of peace promised after the failure of Sennacherib’s invasion. But some, by a slight change in the Hebrew text, would read ‘the world’ instead of ‘quiet’; others interpret the Hebrew text as meaning, ‘I shall be deprived of the sight of man, as those who dwell in quiet (that is, in the grave) are’.
The Hebrew word here used may mean ‘my contemporaries’, or ‘my dwelling’, or perhaps simply ‘my life’.
The Hebrew text appears to mean, ‘I will walk as if in procession all my years in the bitterness of my soul’.
Literally, ‘Lord, if it is thus men live, and the breath of my life is such, thou wilt correct me and make me live’. The Hebrew text has ‘grant me recovery’ instead of ‘correct me’.

We should expect verses 21 and 22 to occur between verse 6 and verse 7 above (as they do in IV Kg. 20). It seems probable that they dropped out by accident, and were replaced here as a footnote to the story.
